design_philosophy.core_loop: Navigate side-scrolling stages, defeat enemy waves using found weapons and magic power-ups, progress toward final confrontation with the Monster
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Resource scarcity (weapon/magic availability) combined with supernatural enemy variety; progression from bare-handed vulnerability to empowered combat
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Young swordsman hero rising to challenge a dark lord's supernatural army; rescue narrative (saving Emily) paired with monster-slaying quest
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Blends Castlevania-style gothic platforming with beat 'em up combat mechanics; enforces weapon/magic discovery as progression gate rather than permanent inventories; supernatural enemy roster (Death, Medusa) signals tonal commitment to horror-action fusion

difficulty.lives_system: Standard NES lives system (inferred from era and genre conventions)
difficulty.checkpointing: Stage-based checkpoint structure typical of NES platformers
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Limited; difficulty appears fixed. Player agency emerges through weapon/magic discovery—progression tied to exploration rather than difficulty selection

progression.upgrade_system: Weapon and magic power-up discovery system; progression from bare-handed combat → clubs/swords → fireball magic. Weapons appear as loot within stages
progression.unlock_mechanism: Stage completion unlocks next linear stage; weapon/magic availability gates combat capabilities within stages
replayability_hooks: Multiple weapon types encourage different playstyles
Mythical boss encounters (Death, Medusa) signal landmark achievements

world_building_techniques: Supernatural enemy roster (Death, Medusa, the Monster) establishes gothic horror setting
Stage progression through raided villages suggests expanding Monster's territory
Emily kidnapping frames rescue narrative as quest motivation
Monster's magic subduing mythical entities hints at dark power escalation

boss_design.philosophy: Iconic supernatural entities serve as stage climaxes; Death and Medusa are explicit boss encounters; final confrontation with the Monster as ultimate goal
notable_bosses: Death (mythical entity subdued by Monster's magic)
Medusa (mythical entity subdued by Monster's magic)

faction_indicators: Player aligned with village against Monster's supernatural army
Monster controls enslaved mythical entities (Death, Medusa)
Emily represents civilian stake in conflict
danger_signals: Kidnapping of Emily signals malevolent intent beyond military conquest
Magic subjugation of ancient mythical powers indicates unprecedented danger
environmental_storytelling: Raided village stages show destruction and displacement
Monster's magic presence implied through supernatural enemy composition
Weapon/magic loot scattered in stages suggests resistance efforts or fallen warriors
